identity.mission: The Quiet Record exists to recover the histories that did not make it into the textbooks — the engineers whose names were stripped from their inventions, the women whose research was credited to their supervisors, the migrations that reshaped cities without anyone noticing, the disasters that were rewritten as accidents, the technologies that almost happened. The channel works from primary sources where possible — letters, ledgers, oral histories, parish records, census data, court records, period photographs, infrastructure surveys, museum collections — and from the specialist historians who have done the patient archival work. Every episode answers one question: what really happened, and why have we forgotten it?
identity.audience: The Quiet Record is built for curious adults between 25 and 65 who watch documentaries by Lucy Worsley, Mary Beard, David Olusoga, Dan Snow, and the older BBC Four lineup. They read longform history journalism in The Atlantic, History Today, and the LRB. They visit museums on holiday and take their time at every exhibit. They are bored by the same nine WWII documentaries and the same six Roman emperors. They want to learn something they did not learn at school — particularly histories that have been told from only one perspective for too long.
identity.mood: lyrical, patient, somber where appropriate, warm where appropriate, evocative, British-documentary
identity.forbidden: nationalist mythmaking, imperial nostalgia, 'great man' framing without examination, romanticising slavery or colonialism, presenting contested histories as settled, glorifying military violence, conspiracy framings of historical events, ethnic stereotyping, presenting one historical group as morally pure and another as monstrous, anachronistic moralising that flattens historical complexity
identity.refs: David Olusoga, Lucy Worsley, Mary Beard, Dan Snow, Bettany Hughes, Janina Ramirez, Michael Wood, Adam Curtis, Ken Burns, BBC Four documentaries, The Rest Is History podcast, History Today, The History of Rome podcast (Mike Duncan)

script.tone:
The writing tone should be that of a senior BBC documentarian who has lived with the subject for years — warm, precise, occasionally lyrical, never sentimental, never breathless. The voice should feel like David Olusoga walking through a building he has researched, or Lucy Worsley turning over an object in her hands, or Michael Wood standing in a landscape and letting the silence do half the work.

The writing is essayistic. Sentences vary in length — some long and lyrical, some short and weighted. Paragraphs breathe. The narration is patient because the history was patient. Things took the time they took.

The script structure mirrors a long-form historical essay:

The object or moment (a specific entry point — a photograph, a ledger line, a building, a name on a memorial)
The larger world (the context: the city, the empire, the century, the technology of the time)
The people (named individuals — researched from primary sources — never composite, never imagined)
The event or change (what actually happened, traced carefully through the record)
The forgetting (how this came to be left out of the standard story, and what that omission has cost)
The recovery (the historians, archivists, descendants, or institutions who brought this back into view)
The meaning (what this changes about how we tell the larger story — stated modestly)

Key tonal characteristics:

Name specific people, dates, places — never vague 'people of the time'
Use primary source quotations when they exist, properly attributed
Distinguish carefully between what we know, what we think we know, and what we are reconstructing from limited evidence
Use hedging language honestly — 'the records suggest' / 'we have no direct testimony but' / 'historians disagree about'
Where the evidence runs out, say so — and find the historian who has thought hardest about why
Where histories have been suppressed, name the suppression specifically (when, by whom, to what end)
When discussing colonised peoples, enslaved people, or marginalised groups, name them — never let them remain a faceless category
When discussing perpetrators, name them too — accountability requires naming
Resist the temptation to find easy heroes — the more interesting figures are usually complicated
Be alert to anachronism — judge historical figures by what was known and contested in their own time, not only ours
Use silence — pauses where the listener can imagine the place

Tone shifts naturally between:

Lyrical and evocative when establishing place and atmosphere
Precise and forensic when working through the documentary record
Quietly humane when introducing the people
Grave when discussing harm done
Warm when discussing what survived
Reflective at the close

The writing should always be:

Grounded in named sources
Generous to historical figures without excusing harm
Clear about contested claims
Alert to whose stories have been left out
Willing to admit uncertainty
Patient — let the history breathe

The writing should never be:

Breathless
Moralising in a way that flattens complexity
Nationalist
Nostalgic for any empire
Presentist (judging the past as if everyone then thought as we think now)
Condescending toward the audience
Reductive ('this is when X really started')
Melodramatic
A history lecture with no atmosphere
A travelogue with no history
script.hook: Start with a specific physical thing — an object, a place, a name on a list. One sentence that grounds the viewer in something tangible from the period. Then the question that the object opens. Example: 'On the third floor of a museum in Lisbon there is a wooden chest. Inside it are eighty-seven letters, written in three different hands, all dated between June and September of 1755. The chest belonged to a woman whose name does not appear in any of the histories of the earthquake. This is how we know she was there.' Establish atmosphere and stakes immediately. The viewer should feel the period and the question at once.
script.subject_areas: Pre-industrial labour history, women in science before 1900, the engineers and clerks whose names are missing from invention histories, the migration histories of single neighbourhoods, the slow disappearance of languages, the women in early computing (beyond the canonical few), Black history in Britain, indigenous histories of places that became European cities, the lives of household servants, the architecture of forgotten infrastructures (sewers, almshouses, lighthouses, lazarettos), industrial disasters that were reclassified as accidents, the histories of asylums and how the patients were named in admission records, lost civic institutions (lending libraries, mutual aid societies, mechanics' institutes), the role of merchant women in early modern Europe, queer histories preserved obliquely in court records, the children's labour at the foundations of industrialisation, the unnamed soldiers whose letters survive in regimental archives, the slow demographic histories that shaped cities (the cholera years, the late-19th-century Atlantic migrations, the post-war housing decisions), the engineering decisions that built cities (London sewers, Parisian water, Mumbai railways, Buenos Aires gridiron), the small disasters that changed regulation (Triangle Shirtwaist, Tay Bridge, Texas City), forgotten famines, forgotten plagues, forgotten massacres, the histories that are missing because the records were deliberately destroyed
script.lesson: Every episode ends with one observation about what the history changes for us — never prescriptive, never a call to action, never 'and this is why we must remember'. Examples: 'Her name appears once, in a parish register, as the witness to a baptism in 1782. Everything else we know about her is reconstructed from what others said. It is not very much. It is more than nothing.' / 'The factory is now a museum. The names of the eleven women who died on the third floor in 1893 are on a small plaque near the lift.' / 'The bridge was rebuilt in 1887. It is still standing. The first one is mostly remembered now as a footnote in a poem.'
script.forbidden_words: great man, civilisation (as a singular noun applied approvingly to one society), savage, primitive, discovered (when European), enlightened (without qualification), our island story, our heritage (as an exclusionary frame), they came / they brought (when referring to colonised peoples as visitors to their own land), founding fathers (in a hagiographic register), shocking, mind-blowing, the truth about, lost to time (overused — be specific), epic, legendary (overused)
script.power_words: ledger, registry, archive, parish record, census, manifest, charter, deed, will, inventory, letter, testimony, deposition, account, almshouse, foundling, manumission, indenture, apprenticeship, guild, livery, freight, cargo, manifest, port, customs, plot, holding, common, enclosure, charter, lease, summons, indictment, plea, verdict, plaque, headstone, memorial, inscription

visuals.vis_palette: Deep brown-black base (#1a1008), warm amber gold (#c8a24b accent), bone white, sepia tones, candlelight orange-yellow, soft archival fade, parchment beige, lamplight halo
visuals.vis_accent_color: Amber gold for emphasis on names, dates, and key inscriptions. Bone white for body text and citations. Sepia warmth throughout archival photography. Candlelight palette in evocative period reconstructions. No bright modern colours — the channel lives in archival warmth.
visuals.vis_broll_rules:
* archival photograph → slow Ken-Burns push into a single sepia or black-and-white image, the subject's name and the year appearing in serif below, photograph held for 6-8 seconds minimum
* document → period document filling the frame — parish register, ledger page, ship's manifest, parliamentary record, deed, will — slow camera move across the page, key line appearing in amber highlight as narrator reads it
* landscape → wide establishing shot of a place as it looks today — patient, unhurried, often with no human in frame — let the landscape carry the time depth
* place then and now → archival photograph or engraving of a location on left half of frame, modern shot of the same location on right half, slow cross-dissolve between them
* hands → close-up of hands engaged in period craft, hands holding an object from the time, fingertips on the edge of a document — the human scale of history
* object → museum specimen photographed in single-source lamplight against dark background, slow rotation or push-in, scale reference and provenance label appearing below
* portrait → painted or photographic portrait of a named individual, slow Ken-Burns, name and dates of life appearing in serif (e.g. 'Sarah Forbes Bonetta, 1843–1880')
* the city as palimpsest → modern street footage with archival overlay fading in to reveal what stood there before — used sparingly, for moments where the layering matters
* the building → architectural exterior at golden hour, slow track or static hold, the building's date and original purpose appearing as a caption
* the interior → period-appropriate interior (museum-grade, never staged historical reenactment) — kitchens, workshops, libraries, almshouses, weaving rooms
* the workshop → real museum reconstructions of period workplaces, period tools, the hands of an experienced demonstrator working in period methods
* the river / coast / port → water that has carried the history — patient, atmospheric, often at dawn or dusk
* the map → period map in slow zoom, contemporary place names appearing in serif as the camera moves across, route or boundary lines highlighted in amber
* the ledger column → close-up of bookkeeping or registry in period hand, single line highlighted, key name or sum visible
* the engraving → period print, woodcut, or engraving, held with respect for the medium, attribution and date below
* archival film → silent newsreel, early documentary footage, oral history footage (when subject and provenance are clear), held without interruption for emotional weight
* oral history audio → archive audio of a named person speaking, played over a still photograph of them, with date of recording captioned
* the museum case → glass display case with single object inside, soft museum light, the object held as a relic of the moment under discussion
* the memorial → plaques, headstones, foundation stones, war memorials, naming walls — slow patient holds, never lingering for melodrama
* candlelight period reconstruction → if reconstruction must be used at all, only in candlelight or low museum light, never with actors playing named historical figures, never with dialogue, only as atmospheric texture
* the historian at work → present-day historian in archive, hands turning a manuscript, working at a research desk — quiet documentary footage of the recovery process
* the descendant → present-day descendant of a historical figure, photographed with respect, named, with their relationship to the historical figure captioned
* maps and migration → period maps with subtle animated arrows showing routes of movement (immigration, trade, displacement) — animation is slow and serif-labeled
* time period — pre-1700 → manuscripts, illuminated pages, panel paintings, woodcuts, parish records in period hand
* time period — 1700-1850 → engravings, early lithographs, painted portraits, period maps, account books, broadsheets
* time period — 1850-1900 → wet-plate photography, daguerreotypes, early newspapers, hand-drafted engineering plans, census ledgers
* time period — 1900-1945 → black-and-white photography, silent and early sound film, period typewriter documents, early radio recordings
* time period — 1945-1980 → 16mm colour archival film with characteristic fade, oral history recordings, period photographs (Kodachrome warmth)
* time period — 1980-present → handheld video, contemporary documentary footage, recent photography, present-day return to historical sites
visuals.vis_forbidden:
* No reenactments with actors playing named historical figures speaking dialogue
* No AI-generated portraits of historical individuals
* No generic CGI castles, ships, or cities for the sake of visual filler
* No 'speed-painted' animated history graphics
* No staged period drama footage from movies or TV (unless legally and contextually appropriate — and always credited)
* No anachronistic stock footage standing in for the period
* No tour-vlog aesthetic
* No historian-on-camera presenter shots done quickly — if a historian appears, they must be properly framed, named, and given time
* No bright daylight tourist footage of historical sites
* No drone footage that becomes the visual centerpiece (drones for context only, never spectacle)
* No Game of Thrones-style golden hour rolling fields
* No 'reconstructed' faces of historical figures unless from properly sourced academic forensic anthropology
* No romanticised imagery of colonisation, empire, or military violence
* No nationalist iconography (flags as triumphal centerpieces, anthems on the soundtrack)
* No 'great man on horseback' framing without examination
* No imagery that exoticises non-European peoples or places
* No stock photography of generic 'historical people' (figures in period costume meant to evoke a time without being specific)
* No moving the camera through CGI period spaces
* No infographic-style animated history explainers
* No 'speed history' video aesthetic
* No imagery of enslaved people that is gratuitous, unattributed, or used as visual texture without context
* No graphic depictions of violence — even when violence is the subject
* No imagery of human remains except in respectful museum context with attribution
* No bright modern colour grading on archival material — let the period look like the period
* No clickbait visual composition
* Every visual should: ground the viewer in the period, illustrate a sourced claim, honour the people in it, or reveal the work of recovery — never substitute for the absent record
visuals.vis_aroll_rule: There is no celebrity face. The narrator (Ryan) is the constant voice. The visual A-roll equivalent is the archive — period documents, photographs, places, objects. The archive is the protagonist of every shot. Hold on a photograph or document for 5-8 seconds while the narrator works through it. Use slow Ken-Burns moves only — never fast pans, never zooms that draw attention to themselves. The viewer should always feel the camera respects the material.

mograph.triggers:
* timeline → horizontal timeline appearing on dark background, dates appearing one by one in serif as narrator names them, key events as small typeset entries with year, the cursor pausing at each entry for 2 seconds
* map → vintage atlas-style map in warm parchment tones, country and city names appearing in serif as narrator references them, subtle amber pin dropping at the geographic anchor of the story, route lines animating in slow amber strokes
* genealogy → family-tree diagram in serif typography on dark parchment, names appearing generation by generation, dates of birth and death beneath each name, key relationships highlighted in amber
* archival document → high-resolution scan of a real period document filling the frame, slow camera tilt down the page, key line being read by narrator highlighted in subtle amber underline
* census or registry → ledger page in period hand, camera moving slowly across the columns, the specific row being discussed illuminating in soft amber, the name being read appearing in serif at lower third
* manifest → ship's or trade manifest, columns of cargo or names, the relevant entry highlighting as narrator names it, geographic origin appearing in serif caption
* migration → animated map showing routes of movement over a period of years, dates appearing as the lines extend, scale of movement (number of people, where known) appearing as serif label
* portrait reveal → archival portrait fading in slowly, name and life dates appearing in serif beneath, profession or role on third line — held for 5-6 seconds total
* before / after → split frame of the same location in two different eras, slow cross-dissolve between them, year labels appearing at the moment of transition
* the historian → present-day historian in archive, captioned with their name, institution, and the relevant book or article they wrote, hands turning a page or working at a desk
* descendant or community member → present-day individual, captioned with their name and relationship to the historical figure or place
* statistical context → simple, elegant bar or line graph in amber on dark background, axes labeled, source citation at lower third
* place name change → city or region shown on a map with its historical name in serif, the modern name fading in beneath, dates of the change appearing as caption
* the building's history → architectural elevation drawing on dark background, key alterations over the centuries appearing in sequence as overlay layers, dates of each phase labeled
* archaeology → archaeological site photograph or trench plan, find labels appearing in serif, year of excavation captioned
* an inscription → close-up of stone inscription, headstone, plaque, or foundation stone, the text being read by narrator, translation appearing in serif if from another language
* a will or legal document → period legal document, key bequest or clause highlighted, beneficiary's name appearing in serif as narrator reads it
* parish record → register page in period clerk's hand, the relevant entry (baptism, marriage, burial) highlighted, the named individual receiving a serif caption with date
* trade route → animated map of a trade or commercial route, with cargo or commodity flow labeled, key ports and dates appearing as labels
* family migration → multi-generational family path traced on map, with birth, marriage, death, migration events labeled at appropriate geographic and temporal points
* the gap in the record → blank parchment with a single date or name, the absence itself emphasised in serif typography — for moments when the record runs out and the silence becomes part of the story
* historians at the archive → present-day footage of a researcher in white gloves handling a manuscript, library stacks behind, the work of recovery made visible
* the museum object → museum object photographed against black with single-source lamplight, slow rotation, provenance and date appearing as serif caption
* what we know / what we don't know → two-column serif typography, left side listing what the record shows, right side listing what remains unknown — calm, honest, never dramatic
* the recovered name → single name appearing in serif at centre of dark frame, with life dates below, profession or role on third line, then a fourth line noting how the name was recovered (which archive, which historian, which descendant)
* a piece of music or poetry from the period → text of a period song lyric, poem, or psalm appearing in serif on dark background, with source and date captioned, narrator may read it
mograph.style:
* Motion graphics should look like the visual language of a refined BBC documentary or a Ken Burns film — patient, lyrical, archival
* Texture is paramount: parchment, ink, period printing imperfections, candlelight warmth, soft archival fade
* Typography is the foundation — use a refined serif (Crimson, Garamond, Sabon, or similar) for body text, captions, and citations
* Never use modern grotesque typography for body text — only for occasional data labels in statistical mographs
* Colour is warm and restrained: amber gold for emphasis, bone white for body, sepia and parchment for archival texture, never bright modern colour
* Animation is slow and considered — texts and graphics fade in, never snap or glitch
* No flashy data visualisation — when statistics appear, they are presented as a refined plate, not as an animated infographic
* Maps are vintage atlas style, never Google Maps, never tech-bro globe animations
* Timelines look like exhibit panels at a museum, not like infographic explainers
* Family trees and genealogies are calligraphic in feel, never tech-tree
* When portraits appear, they are held with reverence — the channel never disrespects the dignity of the subject
* The amber underline is the channel's signature emphasis — used only when narrator quotes verbatim from a source, applied during the spoken line, fades after
* Every mograph should feel like it could appear in a museum exhibit
* The channel is allergic to flashy animation — restraint is the brand
* Camera moves are exclusively slow Ken-Burns pushes and gentle pans — never whip pans, never quick zooms
* The texture of the period must dominate over any modern aesthetic choice
mograph.text_style: Refined serif throughout — Crimson, Garamond, Sabon, or system serif fallback. Body text white. Amber gold (#c8a24b) for emphasis on names, dates, and key quoted passages. Citations appear in serif at lower third in this format: 'Parish register, St Margaret's Westminster, 1782. Item 4.' Hold for 5 seconds. Maximum 10 words per emphasis text event. Words and phrases fade in by line, never letter by letter. The aesthetic is a beautiful exhibit caption, not a YouTube overlay.

sound.mood:
The sound design should feel like sitting in a cathedral nave at dusk, with the distant sound of footsteps on stone, the faint movement of air through old timbers, and a single voice speaking patiently from somewhere just out of sight. The audio reference points are: Ken Burns's documentary scores by Wynton Marsalis and Bobby Horton, Max Richter's Memoryhouse and The Blue Notebooks, Arvo Pärt's choral and string works at low volume, the score of A Field in England, the silent atmospheric work of Brian Eno's Music for Films.

Music must always serve atmosphere and history — never melodrama, never urgency. The viewer should feel that the music is itself an artefact of the period being discussed.

For the first 90 seconds of any video:

* Music is minimal or absent
* Begin with environmental sound — wind across a moor, footsteps in a hall, distant church bell, river, archive room reverb
* The narrator's voice enters in this near-silence
* If music is present, it is a single sustained string note, very low in mix (-20dB)

When music does enter more fully:

* Solo period-appropriate instrument when possible — viola da gamba for early modern subjects, fiddle for 19th century, simple piano for 20th century
* Sparse string ensemble at most — never orchestral
* Subtle drone or harmonium underneath providing harmonic foundation
* No drums for at least the first half of the video
* No modern percussion ever in subjects pre-1900
* Music sits at -16dB to -14dB under voice — present but never competing

For the establishing sections (place and period):

* Period-appropriate instrumental — fiddle, harmonium, viola, simple piano
* Long sustained notes with rests between them
* The viewer should feel the time of the period — never the time of the present

For the documentary record sections:

* Music thins to ambient only — room tone, archive acoustic, distant bell
* Let the documents speak
* Music drops to total silence when narrator reads a verbatim quotation from a primary source

For the human moments (the named people, the descendants, the recovered stories):

* Music re-enters with restraint
* Warm pad underneath simple melodic line
* Allow the music to feel genuinely moved without becoming sentimental

For the meaning section at the close:

* Music thins to a single sustained note or simple piano line
* Final 30 seconds: ambient only
* Last 10 seconds: silence except environmental sound (church bell, river, wind, footsteps)

Avoid completely:

* Cinematic trailer impacts
* Orchestral swells
* Modern electronic music in any pre-1980 context
* Hip-hop beats
* 'Inspiring' chord progressions
* True-crime score elements
* Sound design that tells the viewer how to feel about a historical event
* Heartbeat sound effects
* Whoosh transition sounds
* Bombastic 'epic' music for any historical subject — including military and political history
* Any music that romanticises empire, war, or violence
sound.ambient: Church bell at distance, wind across landscape, footsteps on stone, archive room acoustic, fire crackle when interior, river or coast when waterborne, market square distant when urban — period-appropriate environmental sound throughout
sound.silence: Music drops to total silence whenever narrator reads verbatim from a primary source. Returns gently 1-2 seconds after the quotation ends. Minimum 3 silent quotations per video. Also drops to silence at the moment of the most significant revelation — the named recovered person, the moment of harm or loss, the place where the record runs out.
sound.swells: There are no triumphant swells. There is one moment, typically in the recovery section, where a low string note rises by +2dB and a simple melodic line enters — this is the closest the channel comes to a swell. The video ends in ambient environmental sound — final 10 seconds are bell, wind, river, or footsteps only.
sound.voice_fx: clean, warm room reverb (-22dB return) to suggest the narrator is reading in a hall, a library, or a cathedral — depending on subject. Slight tape warmth in EQ (gentle high-frequency rolloff above 12kHz) to soften any modern broadcast brittleness. No compression beyond gentle leveling.
sound.extra: Soft page turn sound between sections. Faint quill or pen sound when a name appears in serif. Distant bell at the start of every major section. The channel's sonic signature is the ambient environmental sound — every video establishes its place sonically as much as visually.

editing.edit_pacing:
* Cold open (0:00–0:30) = patient, atmospheric

  * Single archival image or single location shot
  * Narrator's voice enters in near-silence with environmental sound
  * Hold on the opening image for 8-12 seconds while the entry point is established
  * No music for first 45 seconds
  * The viewer should feel they have been gently placed somewhere specific in time and place

* Section 1 — The Object or Moment (10% of runtime) = lyrical, evocative

  * Average shot length: 8-12 seconds
  * The specific physical entry point — the photograph, the document, the building, the object
  * Slow Ken-Burns push into the chosen artefact
  * Establish atmosphere — light, period feel, scale
  * Music remains absent or barely audible

* Section 2 — The Larger World (15% of runtime) = establishing, geographic

  * Average shot length: 7-10 seconds
  * Wide shots of landscape, city, region
  * Period maps with slow camera moves
  * Period scale — population, economy, technology of the time
  * Music enters: sparse single instrument, low in mix

* Section 3 — The People (20% of runtime) = humane, named

  * Average shot length: 5-8 seconds
  * Named individuals — portraits, photographs, records of their lives
  * Each named person held for at least 5 seconds with serif caption
  * If a person has no portrait, their record (parish entry, will, letter) stands in for them
  * The narrator works through each named person with respect — never composite, never imagined
  * Music: simple period-appropriate melodic line

* Section 4 — The Event or Change (20% of runtime) = forensic, patient

  * Average shot length: 5-8 seconds
  * Work through the primary source record carefully
  * Documents, letters, ledger entries, court records — held while the narrator reads from them
  * Use the archival document mograph with amber underline on quoted lines
  * Music thins to ambient when reading verbatim from sources

* Section 5 — The Forgetting (10% of runtime) = grave, restrained

  * Average shot length: 8-12 seconds
  * How this history came to be left out — the editorial choices, the institutional gaps, the family silences, the colonial archives that were never built or were deliberately destroyed
  * Show the silence — blank parchment, empty archive shelves, the place where the plaque is not
  * Music drops to silence at the moment of the most significant absence

* Section 6 — The Recovery (15% of runtime) = warm, dignified

  * Average shot length: 6-9 seconds
  * The historians, archivists, descendants, museum curators, oral history projects who brought this back
  * Name them. Show their hands at work. Cite their books and articles.
  * Music re-enters with restraint
  * This is the channel's quiet hopefulness — the recovery is not triumph, but it is something

* Section 7 — The Meaning (10% of runtime) = reflective

  * Average shot length: 10-15 seconds
  * What this changes for how we tell the larger story — stated modestly
  * Return to the entry-point object or place from the opening
  * Music thins to single piano or sustained note
  * Final 30 seconds: ambient only
  * Last 10 seconds: environmental sound only — bell, wind, footsteps, river

* Avoid throughout:

  * Any cut that interrupts the viewer while they are reading a citation
  * Music swells that telegraph what to feel about a historical event
  * Rapid montage — never used in this channel
  * Cutting away from a portrait or document before the viewer has registered it
  * Visual filler — every frame is a source or a place
  * More than 60 seconds without a named source on screen
  * Pacing that suggests urgency where the history was slow
editing.edit_text_rules: Refined serif typography throughout — Crimson, Garamond, Sabon, or similar. Body text bone white. Amber gold (#c8a24b) for emphasis on names, dates, and key quoted passages — never decorative. Citations appear in serif at lower third with full archival reference (e.g. 'Parish register of St Olave Hart Street, 1665. Burial entry 14 September.'). Hold for 5 seconds. Maximum 10 words per emphasis text event. Phrases fade in by line, never letter by letter. The aesthetic is an exhibit caption.
editing.edit_end: Last 40 seconds: return to the opening location or object. The closing reflection is spoken over the held image. Music thins to single sustained note. Last 15 seconds: environmental sound only — a church bell, a river, footsteps in a hall, wind in a landscape. A final line of text appears in serif: 'Sources for this episode in the description.' Hold for 5 seconds. Fade to black with the environmental sound continuing for 3 more seconds.
editing.edit_special: The narrator (Ryan) is the constant voice. The visual protagonist alternates between the archive (documents, photographs, portraits) and the place (landscape, building, room). Every named person appears with a serif caption showing their name, dates of life, and role. Every primary source quoted appears on screen as the original document with amber underline on the quoted line. Section titles appear in serif at lower-left in this format: 'III. The People'. Section titles fade in and out gently. The narrator never refers to themselves, never asks the viewer rhetorical questions, never says 'imagine you...'. The viewer is being shown a history — patiently, carefully, with the dignity that the people in it deserved and did not always receive.

narrator.persona: A British documentarian in his late 40s or 50s, in the tradition of David Olusoga, Michael Wood, or the older Simon Schama. He has spent years in the archive he is talking about. He speaks with patience and a slight quietness that demands the viewer lean in. He never raises his voice. The warmth is real but never sentimental. The gravity is real but never melodramatic. He pauses where the history pauses. He reads quotations with care, slightly more slowly than the narration, with a small register shift to mark the period voice.
narrator.doc_structure: Reads section titles aloud for the major divisions. Reads primary source quotations in a slightly slower register with a 1.5 second pause before and 1.5 seconds after. Pauses 1.5 seconds at section breaks, 1 second between paragraphs, 0.5 seconds between sentences. Allows pauses up to 3 seconds at moments where the silence of the record itself becomes part of the story.

shorts.formula:
FUNDAMENTAL DIFFERENCES FROM LONG-FORM

ELEMENT	LONG-FORM	SHORTS
Aspect ratio	16:9 horizontal	9:16 vertical
Duration	12-20 minutes	50-60 seconds
Hook time	30 seconds (object + question)	0-4 seconds — single object, single name
Intro sequence	Section structure	No sections — single recovered story
Narrator face time	0% (no presenter)	0% — narrator remains voice only
Music volume	Minimal, period-appropriate	Still minimal — single sustained instrument
Captions	Serif, lower third	Centre frame, refined serif, phrase by phrase
Letterboxing	None (clean 16:9)	None — full vertical frame
Film grain	6-8 amount	6-8 amount
Colour grade	Master grade (warm)	Master grade (warm)

SHORTS — STRUCTURE
Every Quiet Record short follows a precise 4-part structure. No deviations.

▸ SECONDS 0-4: The object or the name. Single archival image or document on screen. Narrator names the person or place in one sentence. Period-appropriate environmental sound underneath.
▸ SECONDS 4-20: The context. Who they were, where they were, when. Two or three sentences. Period photograph or document on screen, slowly Ken-Burns'd. Date and place captioned in serif.
▸ SECONDS 20-45: What happened, or what they did. The specific event, contribution, life path, or moment. Use the primary source document with amber underline on quoted lines.
▸ SECONDS 45-60: The recovery — how this came back into the record. Brief mention of the archive, historian, or descendant who brought it forward. Final card in serif: 'Source: [archive or publication, year]'. Fade to environmental sound.

SHORTS — CAPTION STYLE
Captions are essayistic and dignified.
Position: Centre frame for the recovered name, lower third for citations.
Font: Refined serif (Crimson, Garamond, Sabon, or system serif fallback) — never sans-serif.
Size: Body text approximately 7-8% of frame width per line. Smaller than entertainment shorts on purpose — these reward attention.
Animation: Fade in over 12-15 frames. No snap. No glitch. Phrases appear in groups of 2-5 words. The name of the recovered person appears with their dates of life beneath, holding for 4-5 seconds.
Background: No background box. Faint parchment texture beneath. Subtle text shadow only (1px, 30% black).
Colour treatment: Bone white by default. Amber gold (#c8a24b) on names, dates, and quoted passages. Never red, never neon.

SHORTS — SOUND DESIGN
Music level: -18dB to -16dB under voice. Quieter than most channels — the brand is patient atmosphere.
Transition sounds: Soft page-turn sound (-14dB) between sections. Distant church bell at the start. Footsteps on stone occasionally.
Bass boost: None. The channel does not chase phone-speaker punch.
Voice treatment: Warm room reverb (-22dB return). Voice should sound like Ryan is in a hall or a library.
Silence: The final 4-5 seconds are environmental sound only — bell, wind, river, footsteps. The moment of the recovered name is in near-silence. The closing citation is in near-silence.
shorts.hook:
Shorts Hook Rule

The first 1-4 seconds must establish a SPECIFIC entry point — a name, an object, a place — never a generic question
The narrator's voice enters immediately, in near-silence with environmental sound
The name or object must be concrete and grounded in a real archive
Never 'imagine you lived in...' — always 'In a parish register at...'
Hook formulas:
'In the basement of a Glasgow museum is a wooden chest. The name carved into the lid is Marion Macdonald, 1812.'
'There are 47 names on the war memorial in this Yorkshire village. The fourth one is the one that does not match the official record.'
'She was the engineer who designed the foundation of Vauxhall Bridge. Her name appears once, in a contractor's ledger, in 1815.'
'The factory closed in 1973. The women who worked the third floor were named in only one document. This is the document.'
The first frame should be the archival object, photograph, or place — never text shouting at the viewer
The first sound should be the narrator's voice in calm with environmental sound underneath
shorts.visuals:
Shorts Visual Style

Archival photograph or document is the visual anchor for the entire 60 seconds
Portraits and primary sources held with reverence — slow Ken-Burns moves only
Text in refined serif, fading in by phrase
Name of the recovered person appears prominently in serif with dates of life beneath, held for 5 seconds in centre frame
Citation always appears in the final 5 seconds — archive or publication and year
Film grain at 6-8 amount
Letterboxing OFF for vertical — use the full frame, with generous whitespace
No AI-generated portraits, no staged reenactment, no period-drama footage
No rapid cuts — typical short has 4-7 cuts maximum
Final frame: source citation on near-black background, hold 4 seconds, fade with environmental sound continuing
shorts.music: Single period-appropriate instrument enters at second 8 at -18dB — viola for early modern subjects, fiddle for 19th century, simple piano for 20th century. Drops to silence during the moment the name is named. Returns as a single sustained low note in the final 2 seconds before fade. No trending audio. No drums. The Quiet Record shorts will not chase virality — they will earn saves through dignity and atmosphere.

thumbnail.thumb_concept:
Thumbnail Concept Rule

Every thumbnail is composed like a museum exhibit panel or a refined history-magazine cover — History Today, Aeon history, the BBC History Magazine
Use one of three composition types:
  1. The Portrait — single archival photograph or portrait of a named historical figure, slightly off-centre, with refined serif typography giving their name and dates of life
  2. The Document or Object — a single museum object or period document, photographed in warm lamplight against a dark or parchment background, with a single line of serif type stating what it is
  3. The Place — a single atmospheric photograph of a historical location, often at golden hour or in soft archival warmth, with the place name and the date in serif
Lighting: warm amber lamplight from upper left, soft shadow from lower right — the desk-in-a-library or candlelit-museum feel
Texture: parchment, ink, period photographic warmth, sepia where appropriate
The channel name THE QUIET RECORD appears small in lower right, in refined serif, in amber gold
Never exaggerated facial expression
Never clickbait composition
Thumbnails should look like book covers or museum exhibits, not YouTube thumbnails — over time the audience learns to recognise the format and click for the quality signal
thumbnail.thumb_font: Refined serif — Crimson, Garamond, Sabon, or Caslon
thumbnail.thumb_text_formula:
Text Font Style on Thumbnail

Refined serif only — never sans-serif for the main text
Mixed case for the main title, never all-caps shouting
Colour: bone white on dark or parchment background, amber gold (#c8a24b) only for the most important emphasis word
Text size: legible on mobile but breathing — the image is the protagonist
Maximum 5 words plus optional date or name
Format: 'The Woman Who Built Vauxhall Bridge' / 'A Parish Register from 1782' / 'The Names on the Yorkshire Memorial'
No question marks unless they appear in the actual source
No exclamation marks ever
thumbnail.thumb_title_formula:
Title Formula

The woman who designed Vauxhall Bridge
The names on the Yorkshire war memorial
The parish register that survives only because
The engineer who was paid in someone else's name
The factory women named in a single document
A Glasgow ledger and the lives it records
The Lisbon earthquake, in eighty-seven letters
The servants who built the great house
The Black soldiers in the Yorkshire regiment
The London street that vanished in 1864
The migrants who arrived on the third boat
The woman whose name is missing from the patent
A cholera year in one London parish
The forgotten engineer of the Manchester canal
The almshouse register of 1798
The mathematicians at Bletchley nobody named
The widows who ran the dock-side trade
The Quaker schoolteacher of Bristol, 1842
The colonial archive that was burned in 1947
The lighthouse keeper's letters
thumbnail.thumb_forbidden:
Forbidden Thumbnail Elements
No CGI castles or battle scenes
No period-drama film stills used as historical illustration
No AI-generated portraits of historical individuals
No dramatic glaring faces
No clickbait arrows or circles
No gold-tinted royalty imagery without examination
No bright modern colours
No nationalist iconography
No flags used as triumphal centerpieces
No crowns, sceptres, or monarchical regalia as decoration
No more than 5-6 words of text
No screaming, weeping, or contorted expressions
No blood splatter or wound imagery
No lazy 'mysterious figure in cloak' historical stock
No Game of Thrones aesthetic
No Indiana Jones aesthetic
No speed-painted history visuals
No conspiracy 'hidden truth' framing
No emojis
No melodramatic typography
No present-day political imagery imported into historical context

seo.description:
SEO Package Template Rules

SEO title should be specific and human — name the person, place, or document where possible
Main keyword should appear in the first 6 words
Description opens with one sentence stating the archive, source, or historian the episode draws on
Link to source publications, archives, or museum collections in description
Use 6-10 hashtags
Use a mix of broad history tags and specific period or place tags
Include tags related to:
  social history
  microhistory
  women's history
  Black history (when relevant and accurate)
  British history (when relevant — without nationalist framing)
  archival research
  named historians whose work informs the episode
Titles should be under 70 characters when possible
Descriptions should be inviting and substantive, never breathless
CTA should invite viewers to explore the named archives and historians
seo.tags: history, social history, microhistory, archival research, women's history, Black history, British history, local history, oral history, parish records, census, archive, ledger, manifest, primary source, museum collection, family history, genealogy, history of work, history of cities, history of migration, history of medicine, history of technology, history of empire (critical), forgotten histories
seo.cta: Description CTA: 'Sources for this episode — archives, books, and articles — are linked below.' / 'Subscribe for one quiet history per week, drawn from the archive.' / 'Comment with places, people, or archives you would like recovered.' / 'Support the work — every episode is built on weeks in the record.'
